--- SOURCE 1: Type-II superconductor ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Type-II_superconductor

SUMMARY:
A type-II superconductor is a superconductor that exhibits an intermediate phase of mixed ordinary and superconducting properties at intermediate temperature

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Type-II superconductor

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Superconductor_interactions_with_magnetic_field.png/500px-Superconductor_interactions_with_magnetic_field.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/YBCO_vortices.jpg/250px-YBCO_vortices.jpg)

In [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity"), a **type-II superconductor** is a superconductor that exhibits an intermediate phase of mixed ordinary and superconducting properties at intermediate temperature and fields above the superconducting phases.
It also features the formation of [magnetic field vortices](/wiki/Abrikosov_vortex "Abrikosov vortex") with an applied external [magnetic field](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field").
This occurs above a certain critical field strength *Hc1*. The vortex density increases with increasing field strength. At a higher critical field *Hc2*, superconductivity is destroyed. Type-II superconductors do not exhibit a complete [Meissner effect](/wiki/Meissner_effect "Meissner effect").[[2]](#cite_note-Tinkham-2)

## History

In 1935, J.N. Rjabinin and [Lev Shubnikov](/wiki/Lev_Shubnikov "Lev Shubnikov")[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) experimentally discovered the type-II superconductors. In 1950, the theory of the two types of [superconductors](/wiki/Superconductors "Superconductors") was further developed by [Lev Landau](/wiki/Lev_Landau "Lev Landau") and [Vitaly Ginzburg](/wiki/Vitaly_Ginzburg "Vitaly Ginzburg") in their paper on [Ginzburg–Landau theory](/wiki/Ginzburg%E2%80%93Landau_theory "Ginzburg–Landau theory").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In their argument, a [type-I superconductor](/wiki/Type-I_superconductor "Type-I superconductor") had positive [free energy](/wiki/Thermodynamic_free_energy "Thermodynamic free energy") of the superconductor-normal metal boundary. Ginzburg and Landau pointed out the possibility of type-II superconductors that should form inhomogeneous state in strong magnetic fields. However, at that time, all known superconductors were type-I, and they commented that there was no experimental motivation to consider precise structure of type-II superconducting state. The theory for the behavior of the type-II superconducting state in magnetic field was greatly improved by [Alexei Alexeyevich Abrikosov](/wiki/Alexei_Alexeyevich_Abrikosov "Alexei Alexeyevich Abrikosov"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) who was elaborating on the ideas by [Lars Onsager](/wiki/Lars_Onsager "Lars Onsager") and [Richard Feynman](/wiki/Richard_Feynman "Richard Feynman") of quantum vortices in [superfluids](/wiki/Superfluids "Superfluids"). Quantum vortex solution in a superconductor is also very closely related to [Fritz London](/wiki/Fritz_London "Fritz London")'s work on [magnetic flux](/wiki/Magnetic_flux "Magnetic flux") quantization in superconductors. The [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") was awarded for the theory of type-II superconductivity in 2003.[[7]](#cite_note-Abrikosov-7)

## Vortex state

[Ginzburg–Landau theory](/wiki/Ginzburg%E2%80%93Landau_theory "Ginzburg–Landau theory") introduced the [superconducting coherence length](/wiki/Superconducting_coherence_length "Superconducting coherence length") ξ in addition to [London magnetic field penetration depth λ](/wiki/London_penetration_depth "London penetration depth"). According to Ginzburg–Landau theory, in a type-II superconductor 



λ

{\displaystyle \lambda /\xi >1/{\sqrt {2}}}
![{\displaystyle \lambda /\xi >1/{\sqrt {2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fbfbb92caa5a27932d285a56bfd6164dc80ded2e). Ginzburg and Landau showed that this leads to negative energy of the interface between superconducting and normal phases. The existence of the negative interface energy was also known since the mid-1930s from the early works by the London brothers. A negative [interface energy](/wiki/Interface_energy "Interface energy") suggests that the system should be unstable against maximizing the number of such interfaces. This instability was not observed until the experiments of Shubnikov in 1936 where two critical fields were found.

![{\displaystyle \lambda /\xi >1/{\sqrt {2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fbfbb92caa5a27932d285a56bfd6164dc80ded2e)

In 1952 an observation of type-II superconductivity was also reported by Zavaritskii. [Fritz London](/wiki/Fritz_London "Fritz London") demonstrated [[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) that a magnetic flux can penetrate a superconductor via a topological defect that has integer phase winding and carries quantized magnetic flux. Onsager and Feynman demonstrated that quantum vortices should form in superfluids.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A 1957 paper by [A. A. Abrikosov](/wiki/Alexei_Alexeyevich_Abrikosov "Alexei Alexeyevich Abrikosov")[[12]](#cite_note-12) generalizes these ideas. In the limit of very short coherence length the vortex solution is identical to London's fluxoid,[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) where the vortex core is approximated by a sharp cutoff rather than a gradual vanishing of superconducting condensate near the vortex center. Abrikosov found that the vortices arrange themselves into a regular array known as a *vortex lattice*.[[7]](#cite_note-Abrikosov-7) Near a so-called upper critical magnetic field, the problem of a superconductor in an external field is equivalent to the problem of vortex state in a rotating superfluid, discussed by [Lars Onsager](/wiki/Lars_Onsager "Lars Onsager") and [Richard Feynman](/wiki/Richard_Feynman "Richard Feynman").

### Flux pinning

In the vortex state, a phenomenon known as [flux pinning](/wiki/Flux_pinning "Flux pinning") becomes possible. This is not possible with [type-I superconductors](/wiki/Type-I_superconductor "Type-I superconductor"), since they cannot be penetrated by magnetic fields.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

If a superconductor is cooled in a field, the field can be trapped, which can allow the superconductor to be suspended over a magnet, with the potential for a frictionless joint or bearing. The worth of flux pinning is seen through many implementations such as lifts, frictionless joints, and transportation. The thinner the superconducting layer, the stronger the pinning that occurs when exposed to magnetic fields.

## Materials

Type-II superconductors are usually made of metal [alloys](/wiki/Alloys "Alloys") or [complex oxide](/wiki/Complex_oxide "Complex oxide") [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic"). All [high-temperature superconductors](/wiki/High-temperature_superconductors "High-temperature superconductors") are type-II superconductors. While most elemental superconductors are type-I, [niobium](/wiki/Niobium "Niobium"), [vanadium](/wiki/Vanadium "Vanadium"), and [technetium](/wiki/Technetium "Technetium") are elemental type-II superconductors. [Boron](/wiki/Boron "Boron")-doped [diamond](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond") and [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") are also type-II superconductors. Metal alloy superconductors can also exhibit type-II behavior (e.g., [niobium–titanium](/wiki/Niobium%E2%80%93titanium "Niobium–titanium"), one of the most common superconductors in applied superconductivity), as well as intermetallic compounds like [niobium–tin](/wiki/Niobium%E2%80%93tin "Niobium–tin").

Other type-II examples are the [cuprate](/wiki/Cuprate_superconductor "Cuprate superconductor")-[perovskite](/wiki/Perovskite "Perovskite") ceramic materials which have achieved the highest superconducting critical temperatures. These include La1.85Ba0.15CuO4, [BSCCO](/wiki/BSCCO "BSCCO"), and [YBCO](/wiki/YBCO "YBCO") ([Yttrium](/wiki/Yttrium "Yttrium")-[Barium](/wiki/Barium "Barium")-[Copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper")-[Oxide](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen")), which is famous as the first material to achieve superconductivity above the boiling point of [liquid nitrogen](/wiki/Liquid_nitrogen "Liquid nitrogen") (77 K). Due to strong [vortex](/wiki/Abrikosov_vortices "Abrikosov vortices") [pinning](/wiki/Flux_pinning "Flux pinning"), the cuprates are close to [ideally hard superconductors](/wiki/Ideally_hard_superconductor "Ideally hard superconductor").

## Important uses

Strong superconducting electromagnets (used in [MRI](/wiki/MRI "MRI") scanners, [NMR](/wiki/NMR "NMR") machines, and [particle accelerators](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator")) often use coils wound of [niobium-titanium](/wiki/Niobium-titanium "Niobium-titanium") wires or, for higher fields, [niobium-tin](/wiki/Niobium-tin "Niobium-tin") wires. These materials are type-II superconductors with substantial upper critical field *Hc2*, and in contrast to, for example, the cuprate superconductors with even higher *Hc2*, they can be easily machined into wires. Recently, however, 2nd generation superconducting tapes are allowing replacement of cheaper niobium-based wires with much more expensive, but superconductive at much higher temperatures and magnetic fields "2nd generation" tapes.

SUMMARY:
A type-II superconductor is a material that exhibits superconductivity below a certain temperature (T c) and has two critical magnetic fields.

# Type-ii superconductors

## Explore chapters and articles related to this topic

### Concepts and Principal Provisions of Fundamental and Applied Superconductivity

*Published in [V.R. Romanovskii](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=V.R. Romanovskii "Search for more titles by V.R. Romanovskii"), [Basic Macroscopic Principles of Applied Superconductivity](https://www.routledge.com/Basic-Macroscopic-Principles-of-Applied-Superconductivity/Romanovskii/p/book/9781351010160), 2021*

*[V.R. Romanovskii](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=V.R. Romanovskii "Search for more titles by V.R. Romanovskii")*

In nonideal type-II superconductors, the vortexes are pinned by the impurities or defects under the so-called pinning force Fp. The sites, in which the vortices stay at a fixed position, are called pinning centers. There are various types of pinning centers (both naturals and artificially created): zero-dimensional pinning centers like a crystal lattice point defects, the one-dimensional pinning centers like displacement lines, the two-dimensional pinning centers like grain and domain boundaries or surface defects and the three-dimensional pinning centers like artificially created precipitations and columnar defect channels. For the specific current, the Lorentz force overcomes the pinning force and the vortexes start the movement. As a result, a dissipation phenomenon occurs. They will continually move through the superconductor until the vortexes are pinned by other pinning centers. The higher the pinning forces Fp, the higher the current density, which can be passed through the superconductor before the transition to a normal state. In other words, the flux pinning phenomenon determines the critical current of nonideal type-II superconductors. Therefore, the critical current density of nonideal type-II superconductors corresponds to the current when the Lorentz force equals the average pinning force, that is, it is equal to |Jc| = |Fp|/|B|. Besides, the pinning force, the pinning center density also influences the critical current.

### Introduction to Superconducting Devices

*Published in [Raja Sekhar Dondapati](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=Raja Sekhar Dondapati "Search for more titles by Raja Sekhar Dondapati"), [High-Temperature Superconducting Devices for Energy Applications](https://www.routledge.com/High-Temperature-Superconducting-Devices-for-Energy-Applications/Dondapati/p/book/9781003045304), 2020*

*[Raja Sekhar Dondapati](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=Raja Sekhar Dondapati "Search for more titles by Raja Sekhar Dondapati")*

Moreover, for many years, it was assumed that only one type of superconductor existed; however, it was later realized that two distinct types of superconductors exist. The two superconductors shared many properties; however, the most distinguishing feature is the characteristics they exhibit under an external magnetic field. The first kind of superconductor, the Type I superconductors (also known as “soft” superconductors, usually elements), loses its superconducting properties in a relatively weak magnetic field, whereas, Type II superconductors (also known as “hard” superconductors, usually alloys) can withstand very strong magnetic fields before losing superconducting properties. However, few elements are notable exceptions, which include Niobium, Vanadium, and Technetium, which are Type II superconductors. Further discussion on the properties of superconductors is presented in subsequent sections.

### Formulation and Classification of Electronic Devices

*Published in [Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole "Search for more titles by Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole"), [Electronics](https://www.routledge.com/Electronics-from-Classical-to-Quantum/Kolawole/p/book/9781003052913), 2020*

*[Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole](https://www.routledge.com/search?author=Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole "Search for more titles by Michael Olorunfunmi Kolawole")*

Semiconductors are unique materials, usually a solid chemical element—such as silicon (Si), or compound—such as gallium arsenide (GaAs) or indium antimonide (InSb), which can conduct electricity or not under certain conditions. Semiconductor behavior is not restricted to solids: there are liquid conductors, for instance, mercury (Hg) and those regarded as “Type II alloys” superconductors. It should be noted that the resistivity of most metals increases with an increase in temperature and vice versa. As such, there are some metals and chemical compounds whose resistivity becomes zero when their temperature is brought near zero-degree Kelvin (0°K) (i.e. −273°C). {Note that absolute temperature, T, in degree Kelvin (°K), is expressed by = 273 + t, where t =measured temperature in degree Celsius.} At this stage such metals or compounds are said to have attained superconductivity. The transition from normal conductivity to superconductivity takes place almost suddenly over a very narrow range of temperature. Mercury, for example, becomes superconducting at approximately 4.5°K. Type-II superconductors usually exist in a mixed state of normal and superconducting regions [1]. However, because of atomic diffusion, regions with different dopings will mix rapidly and a stable device with an inhomogeneous structure is not possible [2].

### Vibrating reed magnetometer studies of superconducting and magnetic materials

*Published in [Philosophical Magazine](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/TPHM), 2020*

*[L. E. De Long](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=L.%20E.%20De%20Long "Search for more articles by L. E. De Long"), [A. P. Kaphle](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=A.%20P.%20Kaphle "Search for more articles by A. P. Kaphle"), [B. Farmer](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=B.%20Farmer "Search for more articles by B. Farmer")*

When a type II superconductor is cooled into the mixed state in a magnetic field, FL form and are pinned by sample imperfections, leading to anisotropic screening currents. If the sample is configured as a VR, the screening currents can generate strong restoring forces on the VR (e.g. ‘line tension’ or ‘tilt’ effects, in addition to ever-present elastic restoring forces; see Figure 1). Consequently, a strong increase in the VR resonance frequency fo, and a peak in dissipation (measured by 1/Q) are observed as the temperature crosses below the upper critical magnetic field HC2(T) (see Figure 2) [33]. Brandt, Esquinazi and co-workers showed [12–15,17,33–42] detailed models of FL pinning can provide quantitative explanations of the strong resonant frequency shifts and dissipation peaks observed in SC VR in applied magnetic fields. Brandt’s initial calculations applied to VR in a parallel geometry, assuming an extreme-type-II SC material (κ >> 1) with VR length l >> width w >> thickness t. The University of Kentucky Group (UKG) made technical improvements in the RF cavity design of Xiang et al [21] for VRM measurements at low temperatures and in high magnetic fields. This permitted sensitive measurements of FL motion in SC Nb foils and single-crystal NbSe2 [16], and a variety of other complex SC materials to be discussed herein [18–20,44].

### Effective field study of the magnetism and superconductivity in idealised Ising-type X@Y60 endohedral fullerene system

*Published in [Philosophical Magazine](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/TPHM), 2019*

*[Ersin Kantar](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Ersin%20Kantar "Search for more articles by Ersin Kantar")*

On the other hand, the system exhibits the superconducting-like phenomena with antiferromagnetic C–S interactions. In Figure 7, the centre and surface hysteresis loops show different dependencies with antiferromagnetic C–S interactions. In here, superconducting-like phenomena are the consequence of the existence of a centre magnetic atom. In Figure 7(a), the hysteresis loop of the centre displays type II superconductivity behaviour. According to this behaviour, the hysteresis curves exhibit four coercive field points (±HC1 and ±HC2). Therefore, susceptibilities make two peaks at ±HC1 and two broad maximum at ±HC2. We should also mention that the type II superconductors behaviour was obtained for the first time by Abrikosov [72]. Moreover, type II superconductors were presented by the various theoretical models such as Ginzburg–Landau theory [73], Monte Carlo simulations [74, 75], SO(5) theory [76], effective-field theory [66], Emery model within the composite operator method [77] and renormalised mean-field theory [78, 79]. As seen in Figure 7, with the increase of temperature, the HC1 and HC2 values decrease and when the temperature approaches its critical value (TC = 0.60), the centre coercive field illustrates a single point. In T > TC, the susceptibility shows a broad maximum, as seen in Figure 7(b) and (c) [80].

### Study of the critical current density and the thermodynamic critical field in deuterated κ-(BEDT-TTF)2Cu[N(CN)2]Br organic superconductor

*Published in [Phase Transitions](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/GPHT), 2018*

*[Youssef Ait Ahmed](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Youssef%20Ait%20Ahmed "Search for more articles by Youssef Ait Ahmed"), [Ahmed Tirbiyine](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Ahmed%20Tirbiyine "Search for more articles by Ahmed Tirbiyine"), [Ahmed Taoufik](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Ahmed%20Taoufik "Search for more articles by Ahmed Taoufik"), [Hassan El Ouaddi](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Hassan%20El%20Ouaddi "Search for more articles by Hassan El Ouaddi"), [Habiba El Hamidi](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Habiba%20El%20Hamidi "Search for more articles by Habiba El Hamidi"), [Abdelhalim Hafid](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Abdelhalim%20Hafid "Search for more articles by Abdelhalim Hafid"), [Abdelaziz Labrag](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Abdelaziz%20Labrag "Search for more articles by Abdelaziz Labrag"), [Hassan Chaib](https://www.tandfonline.com/action/doSearch?field1=Contrib&text1=Hassan%20Chaib "Search for more articles by Hassan Chaib")*

Type-II superconductors present one capacity to transport a high current density in the presence of a magnetic field. The limiting value of the critical current density is given by the equality of the two opposing forces acting on the flux lines (vortex). These forces are the pinning force due to the spatial evolution of the condensation energy and the Lorentz force exerted by the transport current. The energy is dissipated when the flow lines move. At low temperatures, the quantum fluctuations of the Josephson vortices cause peculiar energy dissipation, and provide strong indications showing that the pancake vortices and pancake vortices melt separately [22].

# Types of Superconducting Materials and Their Applications

## Introduction

Superconducting materials are a remarkable class of materials that exhibit zero electrical resistance and the expulsion of magnetic fields (Meissner effect) when cooled below a critical temperature (Tc). Since their discovery in 1911 by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes, superconductors have revolutionized various fields, from medical imaging to energy transmission. This article will brief the different types of superconducting materials and their diverse applications.

## Types of Superconducting Materials

Superconductors can be broadly classified into two categories based on their critical temperature and magnetic properties: Type I and Type II superconductors. Additionally, they can be categorized based on their composition and discovery timeline, such as conventional, high-temperature, and unconventional superconductors.

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1740706475-normal-classes-of-superconducting-materials.jpg)[1]

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1740706475-normal-classes-of-superconducting-materials.jpg)

### 1. Type I Superconductors

[Type I superconductors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Type-I_superconductor) are primarily pure metals and metalloids that exhibit superconductivity at very low temperatures (typically below 30 K). They are characterized by a sharp transition to the superconducting state and the complete expulsion of magnetic fields (Meissner effect) below their critical magnetic field (Hc). Examples include:

   - Mercury (Hg): The first discovered superconductor with a Tc of 4.2 K.

   - Lead (Pb): Tc of 7.2 K.

   - Aluminum (Al): Tc of 1.2 K.

Type I superconductors are limited in practical applications due to their low critical temperatures and magnetic fields.

### 2. Type II Superconductors

[Type II superconductors](/content/list-of-low-temperature-superconducting-material.html) are more complex materials, including alloys and compounds, that can withstand higher magnetic fields and have higher critical temperatures. They allow magnetic fields to penetrate in the form of quantized vortices, enabling them to remain superconducting at higher fields. Examples include:

   - [Niobium-Titanium](/niobium-titanium-alloy-powder-nb56ti44.html) (NbTi): Widely used in superconducting magnets for MRI machines and particle accelerators.

   - Niobium-Tin (Nb3Sn): Used in high-field magnets due to its higher critical field.

### 3. High-Temperature Superconductors (HTS)

High-temperature superconductors are a class of materials that exhibit superconductivity at temperatures significantly higher than conventional superconductors (above 30 K). They are often copper-oxide (cuprate) based or iron-based materials. Examples include:

   - Yttrium Barium Copper Oxide (YBCO): Tc of 92 K.

   - Bismuth Strontium Calcium Copper Oxide (BSCCO): Tc of 110 K.

   - Iron-Based Superconductors: Discovered in 2008, with Tc up to 55 K.

HTS materials are particularly promising for practical applications due to their ability to operate at liquid nitrogen temperatures (77 K), which is more cost-effective than liquid helium.

### 4. Unconventional Superconductors

Unconventional superconductors do not follow the traditional BCS theory of superconductivity. They include:

   - Heavy Fermion Superconductors: Materials like CeCu2Si2, where electrons behave as if they have extremely high mass.

   - Organic Superconductors: Carbon-based materials that exhibit superconductivity at low temperatures.

   - Magnesium Diboride (MgB2): A simple compound with a Tc of 39 K, bridging the gap between conventional and high-temperature superconductors.

## Applications of Superconducting Materials

The unique properties of superconductors have led to their use in a wide range of applications across various industries. Below are some of the most significant applications:

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1740706510-normal-main-applications-of-superconducting-power.jpg)[2]

![](https://7cad390533514c32acc8-75d23ce06fcfaf780446d85d50c33f7b.ssl.cf6.rackcdn.com/sc/1740706510-normal-main-applications-of-superconducting-power.jpg)

### 1. Medical Imaging

Superconducting magnets are the backbone of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) machines. [Niobium-titanium (NbTi) superconductors](/niobium-titanium-alloy-powder-nb42ti58.html) are commonly used to generate the strong, stable magnetic fields required for high-resolution imaging. The zero resistance of superconductors ensures efficient operation without significant energy loss.

### 2. Particle Accelerators

Superconducting materials are essential in particle accelerators like the [Large Hadron Collider (LHC)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Large_Hadron_Collider) at CERN. Niobium-tin (Nb3Sn) and niobium-titanium (NbTi) magnets are used to create the intense magnetic fields needed to steer and focus particle beams at near-light speeds.

### 3. Energy Transmission

Superconducting cables can transmit electricity with zero resistance, significantly reducing energy losses compared to conventional copper cables. High-temperature superconductors like YBCO are being explored for use in superconducting power grids, which could revolutionize energy distribution.

### 4. Magnetic Levitation (Maglev) Trains

Superconducting magnets enable maglev trains to float above the tracks, eliminating friction and allowing for ultra-high-speed travel. Japanâs SC Maglev train, which uses YBCO superconductors, has achieved speeds exceeding 600 km/h.

### 5. Quantum Computing

Superconducting materials are at the heart of quantum bits (qubits) in quantum computers. The Josephson junction, a device made from two superconductors separated by an insulator, is a key component in superconducting quantum circuits. Companies like IBM and Google are leveraging this technology to build powerful quantum processors.

### 6. Energy Storage

Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES) systems store energy in the magnetic field generated by a superconducting coil. These systems can release large amounts of energy almost instantaneously, making them ideal for stabilizing power grids and providing backup power.

### 7. Scientific Research

Superconducting materials are used in advanced research tools such as [Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices (SQUIDs)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SQUID), which are highly sensitive magnetometers capable of detecting extremely weak magnetic fields. SQUIDs are used in fields ranging from geology to neuroscience.

### 8. Fusion Energy

Superconducting magnets are critical for confining plasma in nuclear fusion reactors, such as the ITER project. High-temperature superconductors like YBCO are being used to develop more efficient and compact fusion reactors.

### 9. Space Exploration

Superconducting materials are used in cryogenic systems for space telescopes and detectors. For example, superconducting detectors are employed in astrophysics to observe faint signals from distant stars and galaxies.

### 10. Industrial Applications

Superconducting materials are used in electric motors and generators for industrial applications. These devices are more efficient and compact than their conventional counterparts, making them ideal for use in wind turbines and electric vehicles.

## Conclusion

[Superconducting materials](/content/list-of-low-temperature-superconducting-material.html) have transformed modern technology. From Type I superconductors to high-temperature and unconventional variants, each class of materials offers unique properties and applications. As research continues to advance, superconductors hold the promise of revolutionizing even more industries. For more advanced materials, please check [**Stanford Advanced Materials (SAM)**](/)**.**

**Reference:**

**[1] Abd-Shukor, R.. High Temperature Superconductors: Materials, Mechanisms and Applications.**

**[2] Chao Yao, Yanwei Ma, Superconducting materials: Challenges and opportunities for large-scale applications, iScience, Volume 24, Issue 6, 2021, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004221005095**

[Previous](https://www.doitpoms.ac.uk/tlplib/superconductivity/bcs.php)[Next](https://www.doitpoms.ac.uk/tlplib/superconductivity/applications.php)
Type I vs Type II
=================

As scientists began to probe the exciting new properties of superconductors, they discovered that superconductors did have limitations apart from a maximum operating temperature. Although currents can flow without any energy dissipation, superconductivity is destroyed by the application of a sufficiently large magnetic field or if the flowing electrical current density exceeds a critical value.

The critical magnetic field depends on how far below the critical temperature the material is. The graph below shows this dependence.

As more superconducting materials were discovered, it was found that they fell into one of two classes, or “Types” with regard to their magnetic properties, and in particular in the way that they expelled magnetic fields. “Type I” superconductors have a sharp transition from the superconducting state where all magnetic flux is expelled to the normal state.

Type II superconductors, on the other hand exhibit similar behaviour by completely excluding a magnetic field below a lower critical field value and becoming normal again at an upper critical field. However, when the magnetic field is between these lower and upper critical fields, the superconductor enters a “_mixed state_” where there is partial penetration of flux. In order to lower the overall magnetic energy, the material allows bundles of flux to penetrate the sample. Within these filaments, the magnetic field is high and the superconductor reverts to normal conducting behaviour. Around each of the filaments is a circulating vortex of screening current which opposes the field inside the core. This arrangement ensures that the material outside these bundles remains in the superconducting state. The graph below shows the differing dependence of magnetic field on temperature which characterises type II superconductors.

The so-called flux vortices often arrange themselves into regular periodic structures. They can be visualised by covering the surface with a coagulation of very fine ferromagnetic particles. The animation below shows a micrograph taken of a type II superconductor in the mixed state and how it arises from the partial penetration of flux.

The sample as a whole continues to have zero resistance as current flows by the easiest path and as there are superconducting regions, current can still flow without energy loss. It must be noted however, that if the vortices move they will dissipate energy. For the superconductor to remain lossless, the vortices must be pinned in place by defects within the crystal structure of the material. Current research aims to understand and pin the resistive motion of flux vortices in applied superconductors, with the aim of creating high critical current materials for applications.

The reason that some superconductors form a mixed state relies on the relationship between the coherence length, **ξ**, and the London penetration depth, λ. As well as being a description of the distance over which Cooper pairs can be considered to be correlated, the coherence length also describes the distance over which the superconductor can be represented by a wavefunction. For reasons we will not go into, if the penetration depth, λ, is greater than the coherence length, **ξ** it is thermodynamically favourable for the magnetic field to penetrate the specimen and it will be type II. This is shown schematically in the diagram below.

![Image 3: Graph of Type 1](https://www.doitpoms.ac.uk/tlplib/superconductivity/images/Type-I.gif)![Image 4: Graph of Type II](https://www.doitpoms.ac.uk/tlplib/superconductivity/images/Type-II.gif)
Relationship between the coherence length and penetration depth for Type I and Type II superconductors

enriched_points.expanded_content: Type II superconductors exhibit a mixed state, allowing partial magnetic field penetration in the form of vortices, maintaining superconductivity under higher magnetic fields.
YBCO (Yttrium Barium Copper Oxide) is a prominent type II superconductor known for its high critical temperature, achieving superconductivity above 77 K, suitable for practical applications.
Niobium-Titanium (NbTi) is a widely used type II superconductor, particularly in superconducting magnets for MRI machines and particle accelerators, due to its favorable properties.
Type II superconductors can operate at higher temperatures compared to Type I, with many high-temperature superconductors (HTS) significantly reducing cooling costs in various applications.
In Type II superconductors, magnetic fields partially penetrate, forming quantized vortices that allow for energy-efficient operation in strong magnetic environments without losing superconductivity.
Type II superconductors like NbTi are essential in MRI machines, providing strong, stable magnetic fields required for high-resolution imaging and efficient operation.
Type II superconductors enable magnetic levitation in trains, eliminating friction and allowing for ultra-high-speed travel, exemplified by Japan's SC Maglev trains utilizing YBCO.
